text.heading: Banker was killed by policeman, says Coroner
text.p: A policeman, Constable Benjamin Oyigie, killed a banker, Modebayo Awosika in cold blood last October 1, a Coroner, Mr Paul Ojo, held yesterday after an inquest.
Ojo said the evidence before him "clearly shows a callous, unprovoked and unjustifiable shooting of a defenceless Nigerian by a Nigerian police officer."

He urged the Lagos State Commissioner of Police, Mr Marvel Akpoyibo, to declare Oyigie wanted and reopen investigation into Awosika’s killing with a view to establishing all those involved in the fatal shooting.
The late Awosika was a Relationship Manager at First Bank of Nigeria Plc, Victoria Island. He was murdered at about 2:00a.m. on October 1 last year.
His Kia Optima car,with Registration No. GG-529-AAA, was burnt near the Lekki Phase 1 Expressway roundabout, on the outskirts of Lagos.
Ojo, a Chief Magistrate, said: "I hold that the deceased was shot by Benjamin Oyigie and that it was clearly a homicidal shooting.
"I agree with the conclusions reached by the pathologist and also from the totality of the evidence before me.
"I hold that the deceased, Modebayo Awosika, died of cerebral disruption with haemorrhage arising from communicated skull fracture due to gunshot injury."

He disagreed with the testimony of another police officer on duty the fateful day with Oyigie, Constable Akpapa David, who said the policemen suspected Awosika to be a robber because he hit their patrol vehicle and sped off.
Ojo said the testimony was baseless because no shots were fired from Awosika’s car, nor did they discover any arms or ammunition in his vehicle after he was killed.
His words: "There is absolutely no evidence before me of the offence the deceased committed except that he hit the police patrol vehicle and tried to escape and that he injured the police driver.

"Interestingly, the police driver that was said to be injured did not appear before me. Shooting of the deceased to death cannot be a proper or commensurate response to the claim of the police concerning the conduct of the deceased on the day of the incident.
"He never raised a weapon at the police officers. The evidence before me clearly establishes that the lives of the policemen on duty on the morning of October 1, 2008 were not in danger."

Ojo made 13 recommendations "to generally serve as a guide to prevent a repeat of such an occurrence," in line with the Administration of Criminal Justice Laws of Lagos State 2007, which has a provision that states: "From and after the coming into operation of this law, no person shall be committed for trial on a coroner’s inquisition."

He recommended that the Police Service Commission (PSC) should ensure that excessive use of force by police officers is not tolerated, and that officers be held accountable for their actions. Those responsible for such "unjustifiable use of force", he advised, must be brought to justice speedily.

He urged the National Assembly to make a law providing for police accountability regarding violent crime control and law enforcement, which should contain provisions ensuring regular publishing and analysis of national data on the use of excessive force.
Ojo admonished the police to put ensure that new entrants into the force undergo psychiatry and psychology tests, and deploy a computerised monitoring system designed to track officers’ performances.

The magistrate added: "There should be a deliberate policy of efficient firearm control in the police force. International human rights standards on the use of force and firearms should be fully incorporated into police codes of conduct and training.
"The police should be required to keep detailed records on the use of force and to report publicly at regular intervals, provide statistical data on shootings and other use of force," Ojo said.

He also urged the police to introduce training programmes designed to minimise and possibly eliminate the risk of unnecessary force and death in common situations like vehicle pursuits and "stop and search".

"The PSC should establish truly effective and independent oversight bodies in the states with orders to investigate and review complaints against the police, as well as broader policy issues and patterns of concern and to issue detailed public reports.

"The oversight bodies should consist of civilian complaint review boards with authority to carry out independent investigations into complaints against the police, or Ombudsman or monitors to review police internal procedures and performance.
"I recommend that persons flagged down at police stop and search points should stop for the police as law abiding citizens," Ojo added.
